frameworks.name: vector-based navigation
frameworks.one_line: navigation uses directional vectors between distal landmarks to guide goal-reaching

frameworks.name: transition-based navigation
frameworks.one_line: navigation uses learned associations between adjacent states to choose next moves

frameworks.name: deep reinforcement learning
frameworks.one_line: meta-trained deep RL agents serve as model of human navigation strategies

frameworks.name: mammalian navigational systems
frameworks.one_line: spatial cells (e.g., place/grid/head-direction) provide neural reference for comparison

core_question: Which strategies do humans use to navigate wholly novel environments toward goals?
key_assumptions: wayfinding combines landmark-vector memory with on-the-fly transition knowledge
deep RL agents meta-trained to find shortest paths can model human strategy use
strategy deployment varies systematically across stages of a journey
manipulations: varied position along the trajectory (stereotyped journey points)
comparison between humans and meta-trained deep RL agents
key_findings: people adaptively mix vector-based and transition-based strategies at stereotyped journey points
meta-trained deep RL agents exhibit near-identical strategy mixtures
RL networks develop units specialized for vector- vs transition-based computations
specialized units' response patterns resemble mammalian navigational system geometries
interpretations: goal-directed navigation in novel settings relies on an adaptive mixture of strategies
shared computational solutions emerge in brains and deep RL agents facing the same task
